content.problemStatement:
Earth has been invaded by man-eating parasites that are indistinguishable from humans. In a desperate attempt to prevent human extinction, NNN ambitious astronauts venture into outer space, to get away from the parasites. The only problem is that there may already be some among them!

The astronauts are numbered 111 to NNN. You receive QQQ statements in total from the astronauts, possibly more than one statement from the same astronaut, possibly none at all from some astronauts. Each statement is one of the following two types:


111 iii jjj, meaning that astronaut iii accuses astronaut jjj of being a parasite. In other words, jjj must be a parasite according to iii.
222 iii jjj, meaning that astronaut iii vouches for astronaut jjj. In other words, jjj must be human according to iii.
It is a known fact that no human will **ever** tell a lie, and no parasite will **ever** tell the truth. Refer to the sample explanation below for further clarification.

You do not know which of the astronauts are parasites. It is possible that they all are. It is also possible that none of them are.

The statements made by the astronauts are said to be consistent if each astronaut can be labelled as either a human or a parasite such that all parasites lie, while all humans tell the truth.

Your task is to determine whether the statements made by the astronauts are consistent. If they are consistent report the maximum possible number of parasites.

Input:

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. The description of each test case is as follows.




The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and QQQ — the number of astronauts and the number of statements.




Each of the next QQQ lines describes a statement and contains three integers typetypetype, iii and jjj — denoting the type of statement, astronaut iii and astronaut jjj. No two statements in a test case are identical to each other.



Output:For each test case, output a single line containing −1-1−1 if the statements are not consistent, otherwise the maximum possible number of parasites.
constraints: 1≤i,j≤N, i≠j1 \leq i, j \leq N, \, i \neq j1≤i,j≤N,i=j
It is guaranteed that the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5⋅1055 \cdot 10^55⋅105 and the sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106.
**Subtask #1 (10 points):** 1≤N≤20,0≤Q≤2⋅N(N−1)1 \leq N \leq 20, 0 \leq Q \leq 2 \cdot N(N-1)1≤N≤20,0≤Q≤2⋅N(N−1). The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 202020.

**Subtask #2 (15 points):** 1≤T,N≤100,N(N−1)≤Q≤2⋅N(N−1)1 \leq T, N \leq 100, N(N-1) \leq Q \leq 2 \cdot N(N-1)1≤T,N≤100,N(N−1)≤Q≤2⋅N(N−1). Additionally, each astronaut makes at least one statement about every other astronaut.
**Subtask #3 (15 points):** original constraints, but in every statement i=1i = 1i=1.
**Subtask #4 (25 points):** original constraints, but in every statement type=1type = 1type=1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Note that any and all statements by an astronaut iii will be true if and only if astronaut iii is human. Any and all statements by an astronaut iii will be false if and only if astronaut iii is a parasite.
Example case 1: It can either be that astronaut 222 is a parasite and astronauts 111 and 333 are humans telling the truth, or astronaut 222 is human and astronauts 111 and 333 are parasites, both lying. The maximum possible number of parasites is thus 222 (as is in the latter case).
Example case 2: Either 111 and 222 are both human, or 111 is human and 222 is parasite, or 222 is human and 111 is parasite, or 111 and 222 are both parasites. All satisfy the conditions.
Example case 3: 111 says 222 is a parasite and at the same time 111 also says 222 is human. The statements are not consistent, so the answer is −1-1−1.

solution.approach:
**Overview:**

We use a DSU data structure with an extra “parity” (or “relative value”) for each astronaut. In this structure, when we merge two sets based on a statement, we record the XOR relationship between the node and its leader. For each statement, we “merge” the two astronauts with the relation:


For a type 1 statement, the required XOR is 111.
For a type 2 statement, the required XOR is 000.
**Methodology:**



**Initialization:**

Each astronaut is in its own set. We maintain an additional array, say `parity[]`, where `parity[x]` gives the parity difference between node xxx and its parent in the DSU tree.




**Union Operation:**

For a statement between iii and jjj with relation rrr (where rrr is 000 for type 2, 111 for type 1):



Find the leaders (and cumulative parity) for iii and jjj.
If both have the same leader, verify that the difference between their parities equals rrr. If not, there is a conflict.
Otherwise, merge the two sets and set the parity appropriately so that the relation holds.



**Counting Parasites:**

Once all assertions are processed:



For each DSU component, compute the number of astronauts that would be parasites when the representative is assigned 000 and when it is assigned 111.
For a node xxx, if the cumulative parity from the representative is ppp, then:

If we choose the representative’s value to be 000, then xxx gets assigned ppp.
If we choose the representative’s value to be 111, then xxx gets assigned 1⊕p1 \oplus p1⊕p.


Parasites are the ones assigned 000. We select the option (over the two choices) that gives the maximum parasite count.



**Edge Case:**

Isolated astronauts (those with no statements) form single-node components. In each case, we can choose to label them as a parasite for maximizing the count.



The DSU with parity approach has nearly constant (amortized) time per union–find operation, which is efficient given the problem constraints.

**Overview:**

In this approach, we model astronauts as nodes in a graph. Every statement provides a symmetric constraint:


For type 1, the connected nodes must have **opposite** labels.
For type 2, the connected nodes must have **the same** label.
Thus, the problem reduces to checking whether each connected component in this graph is bipartite and then computing the maximum number of parasites.

**Methodology:**



**Build the Graph:**

Create an adjacency list where each edge (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is stored with weight rrr:



Weight r=1r = 1r=1 for type 1 (forcing opposite values).
Weight r=0r = 0r=0 for type 2 (forcing the same value).
Notice that even though the original statements are directed, the conditions are symmetric.



**Depth-First Search (or Breadth-First Search):**

For each unvisited node:



Assign an initial value (say, 000 meaning parasite).
Traverse the component using DFS/BFS, assigning to neighbor xxx the value current⊕weightcurrent \oplus \text{weight}current⊕weight.
If a contradiction occurs (i.e. an already assigned node gets a different value), declare the statements inconsistent.



**Counting Parasites:**

After labeling a connected component:



Count the number of nodes with value 000 (parasites) and with value 111 (humans).
Since the entire assignment for a component can be flipped (all 000s become 111s and vice versa), choose the assignment that gives the maximum parasite count.
Sum over all components.



**Edge Case:**

Isolated nodes (which have no edges) can be arbitrarily assigned as parasites.



This DFS/BFS method is straightforward, clear and also runs in O(N+Q)O(N+Q)O(N+Q) time, which is acceptable given the constraints.
